schools.summary: Gill: this was the same day the plague of the flies was removed; Matthew Henry: here is warning given of another plague, the murrain of beasts; the witnesses note God bidding Moses go in to Pharaoh and renew the demand in the name of the LORD God of the Hebrews, that he let his people go to serve him — the persistent, unchanging demand that opens each new plague (God coming again with the same word: 'Go in unto Pharaoh, and tell him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me'; the constancy of the demand, 'Let my people go, that they may serve me,' the same just requirement pressed through plague after plague, that Israel be freed to worship their God; the authority, 'Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews,' that Moses comes not in his own name but with the word of Jehovah, the covenant God; the warning before the blow, that God sends his message and demand before the murrain falls, giving Pharaoh yet another space to obey; the patience of God, who repeats his call to the stubborn, that they be left without excuse).

themes: Go in unto Pharaoh, and tell him, Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews
Let my people go, that they may serve me
the persistent, unchanging demand that opens each new plague
the patience of God, who warns before the blow and repeats his call to the stubborn

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the threat is conditioned, 'if thou refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still,' the plague following on continued refusal; Gill: continue to refuse, as he had done; the witnesses note the LORD setting the condition, 'if thou refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still' — the just ground of the coming judgment, Pharaoh's continued and willful refusal (God making the plague to hang on Pharaoh's own obstinacy: 'if thou refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still'; the conditional form, that the murrain is threatened 'if' Pharaoh 'refuse,' that the judgment was not arbitrary but the just answer to his persistent disobedience, in his own power to avert by obeying; the doubling, 'refuse to let them go, and wilt hold them still,' marking both the negative, he would not release, and the positive, he would keep holding them in bondage, his settled and active resistance; that God deals fairly with Pharaoh, laying the blame and the choice plainly before him, so that the plague, when it came, should be seen to be of his own procuring; that sinners bring God's judgments on themselves by their own obstinate refusal).

themes: For if thou refuse to let them go
and wilt hold them still
the plague conditioned on Pharaoh's own willful refusal
the judgment no arbitrary stroke but his own procuring, in his power to avert

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'the hand of the LORD' is the power of God manifested in judgment; Gill: this hand was stronger than Pharaoh's, with which he held the Israelites; the witnesses note God declaring his hand upon the cattle of Egypt, horses, asses, camels, oxen, and sheep, with a very grievous murrain — the fifth plague, God's mighty hand smiting the wealth of Egypt (God stretching his hand against the beasts: 'the hand of the LORD is upon thy cattle which is in the field... there shall be a very grievous murrain'; the figure, 'the hand of the LORD,' the power of God manifested in judgment, and stronger than Pharaoh's hand, with which he held Israel, so that God's hand should overpower the hand of the oppressor; the breadth, 'upon the horses, upon the asses, upon the camels, upon the oxen, and upon the sheep,' all the kinds of cattle, the riches and labour of Egypt, struck at once; the severity, 'a very grievous murrain,' a deadly pestilence on the beasts, a heavy blow on the substance of the land; that this plague, after those on the bodies and comforts of men, now struck their possessions, God showing his dominion over all and judging Egypt's beast-gods).

themes: Behold, the hand of the LORD is upon thy cattle which is in the field
there shall be a very grievous murrain
the hand of the LORD, stronger than Pharaoh's, smiting all Egypt's cattle
striking the wealth and labour of the land, and judging Egypt's beast-gods

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD would make such a difference and distinction between them that the murrain should not be on the one as on the other; the witnesses note God promising to sever between the cattle of Israel and Egypt, so that nothing of Israel's should die — the discriminating providence of God, marking off his people from the judgment (God drawing a line between his own and the world: 'the LORD shall sever between the cattle of Israel and the cattle of Egypt: and there shall nothing die of all that is the children's of Israel'; the distinction, 'the LORD shall sever,' that God would make such a difference between the two that the murrain should fall on Egypt's cattle and not touch Israel's, a thing plainly beyond nature and showing the hand of God; the completeness of the mercy, 'there shall nothing die of all that is the children's of Israel,' not one beast of Israel's lost, a perfect preservation; the comfort and the proof in it, that the plague was no chance pestilence but God's own act, since it so exactly spared his people and smote his enemies; the great truth, that God knows how to distinguish between the righteous and the wicked, sparing his own in the day of judgment).

debates.question: What does the severing between Israel's and Egypt's cattle show?

sides.position: God's discriminating providence — a distinction plainly beyond nature, proving the plague was no chance pestilence but God's own act; God knows how to distinguish between the righteous and the wicked, sparing his own in the day of judgment

themes: the LORD shall sever between the cattle of Israel and the cattle of Egypt
there shall nothing die of all that is the children's of Israel
God's discriminating providence, marking off his people from the judgment
a distinction beyond nature, God sparing his own in the day of judgment

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'To-morrow' the LORD shall do this thing, a set and definite time named; Gill: God appointed a set time for the coming of this plague, that it might plainly appear it came from him and was not owing to any natural cause; the witnesses note God appointing a set time, that on the morrow he would do this thing in the land — the fixing of the hour beforehand, the proof that the plague was God's own act (God naming the very time: 'the LORD appointed a set time, saying, To morrow the LORD shall do this thing in the land'; the appointing of 'a set time,' that God fixed the exact hour, 'to morrow,' when the murrain should fall; the reason Gill marks, that God set the time beforehand that it might plainly appear it came from him and was not owing to any natural cause, for no natural pestilence comes at a named hour but this should, proving it the act of God; the certainty, that what God foretold to the day he would surely perform, his word and his work agreeing exactly; that God's giving Pharaoh until 'to morrow' was also a space to repent, a last warning before the blow; the sovereignty of God, who orders the times of his judgments).

themes: And the LORD appointed a set time
To morrow the LORD shall do this thing in the land
God fixing the very hour, that the plague might appear his act and not a natural cause
the certainty that what God foretold to the day he would surely perform

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD brought a murrain, a pestilential disease, on the cattle on the morrow, according to the set time; the witnesses note the LORD doing the thing on the morrow, so that all the cattle of Egypt died but not one of Israel's — the exact fulfilment of the word and the perfect distinction kept (God performing precisely what he said: 'the LORD did that thing on the morrow, and all the cattle of Egypt died: but of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one'; the punctual fulfilment, 'the LORD did that thing on the morrow,' at the very time appointed, the murrain falling to the day; the greatness of the stroke, 'all the cattle of Egypt died,' that is, the cattle in the field, a vast destruction of Egypt's wealth and substance; the kept distinction, 'but of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one,' the promise fulfilled to the letter, not a single beast of Israel's lost, a manifest miracle of discrimination; that the exact agreement of the event with the word, and the sparing of Israel, proved beyond doubt that this was the hand of God; that God is faithful both in his threatenings and in his promises).

themes: the LORD did that thing on the morrow, and all the cattle of Egypt died
but of the cattle of the children of Israel died not one
the plague falling exactly as foretold, to the day, the promise kept to the letter
the sparing of Israel proving beyond doubt the hand of God, faithful in threat and promise

schools.summary: Gill: Pharaoh sent messengers to the land of Goshen to see whether the murrain was upon the cattle of Israel or not, and whether any died; the witnesses note Pharaoh sending and finding not one of Israel's cattle dead, yet his heart being hardened so that he would not let the people go — the proof rejected, Pharaoh hardening against the plainest evidence (even the verified miracle not moving him: 'Pharaoh sent, and, behold, there was not one of the cattle of the Israelites dead. And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened'; the inquiry, that Pharaoh 'sent' to Goshen to learn whether Israel's cattle were spared, and found the report true, 'not one... dead,' a fact he himself proved; the dreadful hardening, 'the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people go,' that, even having proved the distinction and so the hand of God, he still would not yield, his obstinacy growing against the clearest light; that he had every means to be convinced, and was in fact convinced of the truth, yet hardened himself against it; the dreadful state of a heart that will not bend even to evidence it cannot deny).

themes: And Pharaoh sent, and, behold, there was not one of the cattle of the Israelites dead
And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, and he did not let the people go
Pharaoh himself verifying the distinction, yet hardening against the plainest evidence
the dreadful state of a heart that will not bend even to truth it cannot deny

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: God said, Take handfuls of ashes of the furnace, and let Moses sprinkle it toward heaven in the sight of Pharaoh; Gill notes this was on the third day of the month Abib; the witnesses note God commanding Moses and Aaron to take handfuls of furnace-ashes and Moses to sprinkle them toward heaven in Pharaoh's sight — the sign of the sixth plague, the ashes cast up to bring the boils (God appointing a new and striking means: 'Take to you handfuls of ashes of the furnace, and let Moses sprinkle it toward the heaven in the sight of Pharaoh'; the means, 'handfuls of ashes of the furnace,' a small and common thing, taken perhaps from a brick-kiln, the very furnaces of Israel's bondage, to be made the instrument of a great plague; the manner, 'let Moses sprinkle it toward the heaven in the sight of Pharaoh,' that Moses, openly before the king, should cast the ashes up into the air; the fitness some find, that the ashes of the furnace, where Israel had toiled, should turn to a plague on their oppressors, a measure of recompense; that God can make the least things the instruments of his judgments, that the power may be seen to be his).

themes: Take to you handfuls of ashes of the furnace
and let Moses sprinkle it toward the heaven in the sight of Pharaoh
God appointing furnace-ashes, perhaps from the brick-kilns of bondage, as the means of the boils
God making the least things the instruments of his judgments, that the power be his

schools.summary: Gill: the ashes, thrown into the air, should be so multiplied and spread as to be over all the land of Egypt, coming down everywhere of a hot and scalding nature; the witnesses note God foretelling that the ashes should become small dust over all Egypt, and be a boil breaking forth with blains on man and beast — the sixth plague, the painful sores on every living body (the cast-up ashes spreading and falling as a plague: 'it shall become small dust in all the land of Egypt, and shall be a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast'; the spreading, 'it shall become small dust in all the land of Egypt,' that the few handfuls, cast up, should be so multiplied and scattered by God as to cover the whole land, falling everywhere of a hot and scalding nature; the effect, 'a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast,' painful inflamed sores, ulcers breaking out with pustules, on the bodies of men and the remaining beasts alike; the universality, 'throughout all the land of Egypt,' that none escaped, the plague reaching every person and creature; that this plague, after the loss of cattle, now touched the bodies of the Egyptians themselves, a nearer and sorer affliction).

themes: it shall become small dust in all the land of Egypt
and shall be a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast
the few handfuls of ash multiplied to cover all Egypt with painful boils
the plague now touching the bodies of the Egyptians themselves, none escaping

schools.summary: Charles Ellicott: Moses sprinkled it up toward heaven, presenting it, as it were, to God, in evidence of his people's wrongs; the 'boil breaking forth with blains' being an inflammation producing pustules; the witnesses note Moses and Aaron taking the ashes, standing before Pharaoh, Moses sprinkling them toward heaven, and the boils breaking forth on man and beast — the open execution of the sixth plague, exactly as appointed (the word fulfilled before the king: 'they took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh; and Moses sprinkled it up toward heaven; and it became a boil breaking forth with blains'; the obedience, that they 'took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh,' doing openly as God commanded; the meaning Ellicott draws from the gesture, that Moses 'sprinkled it up toward heaven, presenting it, as it were, to God, in evidence of his people's wrongs,' appealing to heaven against the oppressor; the immediate effect, 'it became a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast,' the inflamed, ulcerous sores at once breaking out on all, the plague following instantly on the sign; that God performed exactly and at once what he had threatened, his word and work agreeing).

themes: they took ashes of the furnace, and stood before Pharaoh; and Moses sprinkled it up toward heaven
it became a boil breaking forth with blains upon man, and upon beast
the sign performed openly, the ashes sprinkled as if presenting them to God
the boils at once breaking forth, God performing exactly and instantly what he threatened

schools.summary: Gill: the boils were upon them as on others, which with all their art they could not keep off; they were so sore and painful that they were obliged to withdraw and could not stand their ground; the witnesses note the magicians being unable to stand before Moses because of the boils, which were upon them and all the Egyptians — the final defeat and shame of the magicians, smitten by the very plague (the sorcerers who had withstood Moses themselves struck down: 'the magicians could not stand before Moses because of the boils; for the boil was upon the magicians, and upon all the Egyptians'; the utter overthrow of the magicians, that they 'could not stand before Moses,' not now even to imitate but driven from his presence, obliged to withdraw, their art helpless to ward off the plague from their own bodies; the special note, 'the boil was upon the magicians,' that they who had contested with God's servant were themselves smitten, covered with the painful sores like all the rest, their pretended power exposed as nothing; the justice and the shame, that the leaders of Egypt's resistance were now the open trophies of God's victory, unable so much as to stand; that God brings to shame all the powers that set themselves against him).

themes: the magicians could not stand before Moses because of the boils
for the boil was upon the magicians, and upon all the Egyptians
the final defeat and shame of the magicians, smitten by the plague their art could not ward off
God bringing to shame the powers that set themselves against him

schools.summary: Gill: Pharaoh having often and so long hardened his own heart, God now gave him up to judicial hardness of heart, to his own corruptions, the temptations of Satan, and the lying magicians about him, to make an ill use of everything; the witnesses note the LORD hardening the heart of Pharaoh, so that he hearkened not, as God had spoken — the first plain word that God hardened Pharaoh, a judicial giving-up after long self-hardening (the man having hardened himself again and again, God now hardening him: 'the LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he hearkened not unto them; as the LORD had spoken'; the solemn note the commentators mark, that here for the first time it is said plainly 'the LORD hardened' Pharaoh, where before he had hardened 'his own heart'; the careful sense in which this judicial hardening is understood, that, Pharaoh having often and so long hardened his own heart, God gave him up to judicial hardness, not by infusing any sin but by withdrawing restraining grace and leaving him to his own corruptions, the temptations of Satan, and the lying magicians, so that he made an ill use of all; that this is the righteous judgment of God, who gives men up to the hardness they have chosen, yet without himself being the author of their sin; the fulfilment, 'as the LORD had spoken unto Moses,' that the foretold hardening came to pass).

debates.question: In what sense did 'the LORD harden' Pharaoh's heart (the first time so stated)?

sides.position: A judicial hardening — after Pharaoh had often and long hardened his own heart, God gave him up to the hardness he had chosen, not by infusing any sin but by withdrawing restraining grace and leaving him to his own corruptions, Satan, and the lying magicians; the righteous judgment of God, who is not the author of his sin

themes: And the LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh, and he hearkened not unto them
as the LORD had spoken unto Moses
the first plain word that the LORD hardened Pharaoh, a judicial giving-up after long self-hardening
God not infusing sin but withdrawing grace, the righteous judgment that gives men up to chosen hardness

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: here is a general declaration of the wrath of God against Pharaoh for his obstinacy; Gill: Pharaoh used to rise early, so it was a fit time to meet him; the witnesses note God bidding Moses rise early, stand before Pharaoh, and again demand in the name of the LORD God of the Hebrews that he let the people go — the renewed and solemn demand opening the seventh plague (God pressing the same word with new weight: 'Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh... Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me'; the charge, 'Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh,' that Moses should meet the king betimes, when it seems he went out early, losing no time in pressing God's claim; the same authority and demand, 'Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me,' the unchanging requirement, now backed by the threat of the heaviest plagues; that this opening is followed by a general declaration of the wrath of God against Pharaoh's obstinacy, the most solemn of the warnings; the patience of God yet again, warning before the sorest stroke).

themes: Rise up early in the morning, and stand before Pharaoh
Thus saith the LORD God of the Hebrews, Let my people go, that they may serve me
the renewed demand opening the most solemn warning of all
the patience of God, warning before the sorest stroke against Pharaoh's obstinacy

schools.summary: Gill: this means rather all the plagues yet to come, especially as the hail was the most terrible to Pharaoh; the design being that he might know none is like God in all the earth; the witnesses note God declaring he would send all his plagues upon Pharaoh's heart, his servants, and his people, that he might know there is none like God in all the earth — the threat of the full weight of judgment, with its great design (God warning of heavier strokes to come: 'I will at this time send all my plagues upon thine heart... that thou mayest know that there is none like me in all the earth'; the threat, 'all my plagues upon thine heart,' that the remaining and sorer plagues should now come, reaching even to Pharaoh's heart, the seat of his pride, to break his obstinacy; the breadth, 'and upon thy servants, and upon thy people,' that the whole nation should share the judgment with the king; the great design, 'that thou mayest know that there is none like me in all the earth,' that the plagues were sent to teach Pharaoh and Egypt the incomparable greatness of God, above all the gods they worshipped, there being none like him; that God's judgments are meant to make men know his supremacy).

themes: I will at this time send all my plagues upon thine heart
that thou mayest know that there is none like me in all the earth
the threat of the full weight of judgment, even upon Pharaoh's heart
God's judgments meant to teach his incomparable supremacy above all idols

schools.summary: Gill: these words are rather to be rendered not in the future but that God might have stretched out his hand and smitten Pharaoh with pestilence, cutting him off, yet he had not done so; the witnesses note God saying he could have stretched out his hand and smitten Pharaoh and his people with pestilence, cutting them off from the earth — the declaration of God's power to have destroyed Pharaoh long since (God showing what he might justly have done: 'For now I will stretch out my hand, that I may smite thee and thy people with pestilence; and thou shalt be cut off from the earth'; the better rendering the commentators give, that the sense is not that God now would do this but that he might have done it, 'I might have stretched out my hand... and thou shouldest have been cut off,' for God had not in fact destroyed Pharaoh by pestilence; the point of it, that God could justly have smitten Pharaoh and his people with a deadly plague and cut them off from the earth long before, blotting them out at a stroke; that this sets up the wonder of the next verse, that God had not done so but had spared and preserved Pharaoh for a further purpose; the truth of God's sovereign power and right over the life of the proudest king).

debates.question: How is 'For now I will stretch out my hand... and thou shalt be cut off' best understood?

sides.position: Not as future, but as what God might justly have done — 'I might have stretched out my hand and cut thee off' — for he had not in fact destroyed Pharaoh by pestilence; this sets up the next verse, that God spared him for a further purpose

themes: For now I will stretch out my hand, that I may smite thee and thy people with pestilence
and thou shalt be cut off from the earth
God's declaration of what he might justly have done, cutting Pharaoh off long since
setting up the wonder that God spared him for a further purpose

schools.summary: Gill: instead of cutting thee off, 'I have raised thee up,' made thee to stand and continue in being, preserved thee from perishing by the former plagues, and reserved thee for greater judgments, that my power and name might be shown; the witnesses note God declaring that for this cause he had raised Pharaoh up, to show in him his power and that his name might be declared throughout all the earth — the supreme statement of God's sovereign purpose in Pharaoh (God revealing the end of his sparing and using the proud king: 'for this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power; and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth'; the sense of 'raised thee up,' that, instead of cutting Pharaoh off, God had made thee to stand, to continue in being, preserved him through the plagues and reserved him as the stage on which to display his power; the twofold purpose, 'to shew in thee my power,' that God's mighty hand might be seen in the judgments wrought on so great and stubborn a king, and 'that my name may be declared throughout all the earth,' that the fame of God's power should spread to all nations and ages; the great use the Apostle Paul makes of this verse, proving God's sovereign right to deal with men as he will, to show his power and glory; that God overrules even the rebellion of the wicked to the spreading of his own glory).

debates.question: What is the purpose for which God 'raised up' Pharaoh?

sides.position: That God might show his power in him and declare his name throughout all the earth — instead of cutting Pharaoh off, God preserved and reserved him as the stage on which to display his mighty hand; the verse Paul cites (Romans 9:17) to prove God's sovereign right to deal with men as he will, overruling even the rebellion of the wicked to his own glory

themes: for this cause have I raised thee up, for to shew in thee my power
and that my name may be declared throughout all the earth
the supreme statement of God's sovereign purpose, preserving Pharaoh as the stage for his power
the very verse Paul cites (Romans 9:17) to prove God's sovereign right

schools.summary: Gill: Pharaoh exalted himself against God's people, and so against God himself, disobeying his commands, despising his messengers, and slighting his miracles, showing intolerable pride and insolence; the witnesses note God charging Pharaoh with still exalting himself against his people, refusing to let them go — the rebuke of Pharaoh's proud and insolent obstinacy (God laying bare the root of Pharaoh's sin: 'As yet exaltest thou thyself against my people, that thou wilt not let them go?'; the charge of pride, 'exaltest thou thyself against my people,' that Pharaoh, in keeping Israel and resisting their release, lifted himself up against God's own people, and so against God himself, in intolerable pride and insolence; the 'as yet,' that, after so many plagues and proofs, Pharaoh still persisted, his pride unbroken, exalting himself against the God who had so plainly shown his power; the heart of his sin, that it was not weakness but pride, a willful exalting of himself against the Almighty, disobeying his commands, despising his messengers, and slighting his miracles; that pride against God and his people is a great and provoking sin, drawing down the sorest judgments).

themes: As yet exaltest thou thyself against my people
that thou wilt not let them go?
God rebuking Pharaoh's proud self-exalting against his people and himself
pride against God a great and provoking sin, drawing down the sorest judgments

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: God said, To morrow about this time I will cause it to rain a very grievous hail, such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now; the witnesses note God foretelling that on the morrow he would rain a very grievous hail, such as had never been in Egypt — the announcement of the seventh plague, unmatched in all Egypt's history (God naming the time and the unequalled greatness of the hail: 'to morrow about this time I will cause it to rain a very grievous hail, such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now'; the set time again, 'to morrow about this time,' that God fixed the very hour, a space for Pharaoh to repent and a proof it should be God's act; the greatness, 'a very grievous hail,' a storm of hail with thunder and lightning and rain, the most terrible of the plagues, that as Aben Ezra noted more terrified Pharaoh than any other; the unequalled measure, 'such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now,' a hail beyond any in all the long history of the land, which, lying in a rainless region, scarcely knew hail at all; that God can arm the very heavens against his enemies, the elements his soldiers).

themes: Behold, to morrow about this time I will cause it to rain a very grievous hail
such as hath not been in Egypt since the foundation thereof even until now
God announcing a hail more grievous than any in Egypt's history
the most terrible of the plagues, God arming the very heavens against his enemies

schools.summary: Gill: this is said to denote both the certainty of the plague and its terribleness, that all, both man and beast, found in the field should die; the witnesses note God bidding Pharaoh send and gather his cattle and all in the field, for the hail should kill whatever was left out — the mercy mingled with judgment, a warning by which any might be spared (God, even in wrath, giving a way of escape: 'Send therefore now, and gather thy cattle... for upon every man and beast which shall be found in the field... the hail shall come down upon them, and they shall die'; the warning, 'Send therefore now, and gather thy cattle, and all that thou hast in the field,' that God gives Pharaoh and the Egyptians notice to bring in their men and beasts to safety before the storm; the certainty and terribleness, 'upon every man and beast which shall be found in the field... the hail shall come down upon them, and they shall die,' that whatever was left out should surely perish; the mercy in the warning, that God, in the midst of judgment, opened a door of escape to any who would heed his word, sparing those who took shelter; that God is gracious even in his severest judgments, willing that men should be spared if they will hear).

themes: Send therefore now, and gather thy cattle, and all that thou hast in the field
the hail shall come down upon them, and they shall die
God's merciful warning, a door of escape opened to any who would heed
God gracious even in his severest judgments, willing that men be spared if they will hear

schools.summary: Gill: he that feared the word of the LORD, who, even if not a sincere proselyte, yet had a servile fear of him and dreaded his threatening, made his servants and cattle flee into the houses; the witnesses note that he among Pharaoh's servants who feared the word of the LORD made his servants and cattle flee into the houses — the wisdom and safety of those who heeded the warning (the fearing heeded and were spared: 'He that feared the word of the LORD among the servants of Pharaoh made his servants and his cattle flee into the houses'; the description, 'he that feared the word of the LORD,' that some, even among the Egyptians and Pharaoh's own servants, had learned by the former plagues to fear God's word, whether from true faith or at least a servile fear and dread of his threatening; their prudent action, 'made his servants and his cattle flee into the houses,' that, believing the warning, they brought in their men and beasts to shelter and so saved them; the lesson the commentators draw, that to fear and heed God's word is wisdom and safety, while to despise it is ruin; that even a little fear of God, that takes his word seriously, brings benefit).

themes: He that feared the word of the LORD among the servants of Pharaoh
made his servants and his cattle flee into the houses
those who feared the word bringing their own to shelter and so saving them
to fear and heed God's word is wisdom and safety; even a little fear brings benefit

schools.summary: Gill: he that set not his heart unto it took no notice of the word but treated it with the utmost contempt, and of this sort there were the far greatest number; the witnesses note that he who regarded not the word of the LORD left his servants and cattle in the field — the folly and loss of those who despised the warning (the careless losing what they would not shelter: 'he that regarded not the word of the LORD left his servants and his cattle in the field'; the contempt, 'he that regarded not the word of the LORD,' that he set not his heart unto it, gave it no thought, but slighted and despised the warning of God; that the far greatest number were of this careless sort, refusing to believe or heed; the result, 'left his servants and his cattle in the field,' that, despising the word, they took no shelter and so lost their men and beasts in the hail; the solemn lesson the commentators draw, the contrast between the two sorts, that those who feared the word were saved and those who despised it suffered loss, as ever between the believing and the unbelieving; the just reward of contempt for God's word).

themes: And he that regarded not the word of the LORD
left his servants and his cattle in the field
those who despised the word leaving their own exposed and losing them
the contrast between those who feared the word and were saved, and those who despised it and suffered loss

schools.summary: Matthew Henry: the threatened plague of hail is here summoned by the powerful hand and rod of Moses, and it obeys; the witnesses note God bidding Moses stretch his hand toward heaven, that hail might fall on man, beast, and herb throughout all Egypt — the calling-down of the hail at God's word by Moses' hand (God commanding the storm by his servant: 'Stretch forth thine hand toward heaven, that there may be hail in all the land of Egypt, upon man, and upon beast, and upon every herb of the field'; the command, 'Stretch forth thine hand toward heaven,' that Moses, lifting his hand and rod to the sky, should be the appointed signal at which God would send the hail, the plague summoned by the powerful hand and rod of Moses; the breadth, 'upon man, and upon beast, and upon every herb of the field, throughout the land of Egypt,' that the hail should smite all, men and beasts left out, and the very plants of the field, over the whole land; the obedience of the elements, that the heavens obey God's word given through his servant, the storm coming at the lifting of a hand; the power of God over all creation, who arms the sky against the rebel).

themes: Stretch forth thine hand toward heaven, that there may be hail in all the land of Egypt
upon man, and upon beast, and upon every herb of the field, throughout the land of Egypt
God commanding the hail by Moses' outstretched hand
the obedience of the elements to God's word through his servant, his power over all creation

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: 'the LORD sent thunder,' literally 'voices,' the thunderings being as the voice of God; the witnesses note Moses stretching forth his rod, and the LORD sending thunder, hail, and fire running along the ground, raining hail upon Egypt — the terrible storm executed by God at Moses' sign (the heavens breaking loose at the lifting of the rod: 'Moses stretched forth his rod toward heaven: and the LORD sent thunder and hail, and the fire ran along upon the ground'; the act, 'Moses stretched forth his rod toward heaven,' the same rod, now in Moses' hand, lifted as the signal; the dreadful answer of heaven, 'the LORD sent thunder and hail, and the fire ran along upon the ground,' that God sent at once thunder, literally voices, the thunderings as the voice of God, hail, and fire, lightning, that ran along upon the ground, a storm of overwhelming terror; that it was 'the LORD' who sent it, the act ascribed to God though wrought at Moses' sign, the glory his; that God arms thunder, hail, and fire as his hosts against the proud).

themes: Moses stretched forth his rod toward heaven: and the LORD sent thunder and hail, and the fire ran along upon the ground
and the LORD rained hail upon the land of Egypt
the terrible storm of thunder, hail, and fire executed by God at Moses' sign
the act ascribed to God, the glory his, arming the elements as his hosts

schools.summary: Gill: the hail and fire mingled was a miracle within a miracle, as Aben Ezra observes, that the hail did not quench the fire, nor the fire melt the hail; the witnesses note the hail and fire mingled together, very grievous, such as had never been in Egypt since it became a nation — the unmatched wonder of the storm, fire and ice together (God working a marvel within the marvel: 'there was hail, and fire mingled with the hail, very grievous, such as there was none like it in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation'; the great wonder, 'fire mingled with the hail,' a miracle within a miracle, that the contrary elements, fire and ice, should be joined, the hail not quenching the fire nor the fire melting the hail, each kept by God in its force against nature; the severity, 'very grievous,' the most terrible of storms; the unequalled measure, 'such as there was none like it in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation,' a storm beyond any in all the history of that land, fulfilling exactly the threat; that God can make even the warring elements serve together at his command, marvellously displaying his power).

debates.question: What is remarkable about the fire mingled with the hail?

sides.position: A miracle within a miracle (Aben Ezra) — the contrary elements of fire and ice joined together, the hail not quenching the fire nor the fire melting the hail, each kept by God in its force against nature, displaying his power over creation

themes: So there was hail, and fire mingled with the hail, very grievous
such as there was none like it in all the land of Egypt since it became a nation
the hail and fire mingled, a miracle within a miracle
God making even the warring elements serve together at his command

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch: the hail smote throughout all the land of Egypt all that was in the field, both man and beast, and smote every herb and brake every tree; the witnesses note the hail smiting all that was in the field throughout Egypt, man, beast, herb, and tree — the universal destruction wrought by the storm (the hail sparing nothing left in the open: 'the hail smote throughout all the land of Egypt all that was in the field, both man and beast; and the hail smote every herb of the field, and brake every tree of the field'; the breadth, 'throughout all the land of Egypt,' save Goshen, the storm covering the whole land; the completeness, 'all that was in the field, both man and beast,' that every man and beast left out, as the careless had left them, was slain; the ruin of the crops and trees, 'smote every herb of the field, and brake every tree of the field,' the plants beaten down and the very trees broken by the violence of the hail, a vast destruction of Egypt's produce and substance; that God's judgment, when it falls, is thorough, sparing nothing that is exposed to it).

themes: the hail smote throughout all the land of Egypt all that was in the field, both man and beast
and the hail smote every herb of the field, and brake every tree of the field
the hail smiting all left in the field, a vast destruction of Egypt's substance
God's judgment, when it falls, thorough, sparing nothing exposed to it

schools.summary: Gill: only in the land of Goshen, where the children of Israel were, was there no hail, so that such Egyptians as might dwell among them, with their servants, cattle, and fruits, escaped this plague; the witnesses note that only in Goshen, where Israel dwelt, there was no hail — the gracious distinction again, God sparing his people in the place of judgment (God drawing the line once more between his own and the world: 'Only in the land of Goshen, where the children of Israel were, was there no hail'; the exception, 'Only in the land of Goshen... was there no hail,' that the storm that smote all Egypt fell not at all on Goshen where Israel dwelt, a clear and gracious distinction; the wonder of it, that the same sky should rain destruction on one part of the land and spare another, a thing plainly of God and not of nature; that even the Egyptians who dwelt among the Israelites, with their servants, cattle, and fruits, escaped, sheltered as it were under the covering of God's people; the comfort, that God knows how to preserve his own in the midst of judgment, making a difference between the righteous and the wicked; the type the commentators draw, of the safety of the church in the day of God's wrath).

themes: Only in the land of Goshen, where the children of Israel were
was there no hail
the gracious distinction sparing Israel in the place of judgment
the same sky sparing one part and smiting another, a type of the church's safety in the day of wrath

schools.summary: Gill: Pharaoh sent for Moses and Aaron, under the terror of the storm, and made this confession; the witnesses note Pharaoh, under the storm, sending for Moses and Aaron and confessing that he had sinned, that the LORD was righteous, and he and his people wicked — the forced confession wrung from Pharaoh by the terror of the hail (fear extorting a confession from the proud king: 'I have sinned this time: the LORD is righteous, and I and my people are wicked'; the words, full in sound, 'I have sinned this time: the LORD is righteous, and I and my people are wicked,' owning his sin, justifying God, and condemning himself and his people; that this seemed a fair repentance, yet was wrung from him only by the terror of the storm, not from a softened heart; the commentators' caution, that a confession forced by fear of judgment is not true repentance, as the event proved, for when the plague was removed his heart hardened again; the difference between the terror of the wicked under the rod and the godly sorrow of the penitent; that many will confess sin under affliction who return to it when the affliction is past).

debates.question: Is Pharaoh's confession 'I have sinned... the LORD is righteous' true repentance?

sides.position: No — fair in sound, but wrung from him only by the terror of the storm, not from a softened heart; a confession forced by fear of judgment is not true repentance, as the event proved when his heart hardened again upon the plague's removal (the terror of the wicked, not the godly sorrow of the penitent)

themes: I have sinned this time: the LORD is righteous
and I and my people are wicked
Pharaoh's confession under the storm, fair in sound yet wrung from him by terror
a confession forced by fear of judgment is not true repentance, as the event proved

schools.summary: Gill: 'entreat the LORD, for it is enough,' there was hail, thunder, and lightning enough, or pray that this may be thought sufficient and that there be no more; the witnesses note Pharaoh begging Moses to entreat the LORD to end the thunder and hail, and promising to let them go and stay no longer — the cry for relief and the promise made under terror (the king pleading for the plague to cease and promising release: 'Entreat the LORD (for it is enough)... and I will let you go, and ye shall stay no longer'; the urgent plea, 'Entreat the LORD,' that Pharaoh, terrified, owns the LORD's power and begs Moses to pray for him, confessing he cannot bear the storm; the cry 'it is enough,' that he had had hail, thunder, and lightning enough and could endure no more; the promise, 'I will let you go, and ye shall stay no longer,' a full and absolute promise, made under the rod, to release Israel at once; the commentators' caution again, that such promises wrung out by present terror are seldom kept, the heart returning to its hardness when the fear is gone; that the wicked will promise anything under judgment, yet forget it when delivered).

themes: Entreat the LORD (for it is enough) that there be no more mighty thunderings and hail
and I will let you go, and ye shall stay no longer
Pharaoh's cry for relief and full promise of release, made under terror
such promises wrung out by present terror are seldom kept

schools.summary: Gill: Moses would go out of the city to pray, the city being full of idols, and spread abroad his hands to the LORD; the witnesses note Moses saying that, gone out of the city, he would spread his hands to the LORD and the thunder and hail should cease, that Pharaoh might know the earth is the LORD's — the assurance of answered prayer and its great design (Moses promising the storm shall cease at his prayer, to teach Pharaoh God's dominion: 'I will spread abroad my hands unto the LORD; and the thunder shall cease... that thou mayest know how that the earth is the LORD's'; the going forth, 'as soon as I am gone out of the city,' that Moses would pray apart, outside the idol-filled city; the gesture, 'I will spread abroad my hands unto the LORD,' the posture of earnest prayer; the confident promise, 'the thunder shall cease, neither shall there be any more hail,' that at his prayer God would at once still the storm, a proof of God's hearing his servant; the great design, 'that thou mayest know how that the earth is the LORD's,' that the sudden ceasing, as the sudden coming, should teach Pharaoh that the whole earth, with all its elements, belongs to and obeys the LORD; that God begins and ends the storm to declare his sovereign dominion).

themes: I will spread abroad my hands unto the LORD; and the thunder shall cease
that thou mayest know how that the earth is the LORD's
Moses promising the storm should cease at his prayer, to teach God's dominion
the whole earth and its elements belong to and obey the LORD

schools.summary: Gill: notwithstanding the confession of sin Pharaoh had made and his earnest request, Moses knew he and his servants would not yet fear God; the witnesses note Moses telling Pharaoh that he knew he and his servants would not yet fear the LORD God — the discernment of Moses, seeing through the hollow confession to the unchanged heart (Moses reading Pharaoh's false repentance: 'as for thee and thy servants, I know that ye will not yet fear the LORD God'; the discernment, 'I know that ye will not yet fear the LORD God,' that Moses, not deceived by Pharaoh's fair words, saw that his confession was forced and his heart unchanged, and foretold that he would not truly fear God even now; the meaning of 'fear the LORD,' the true reverence and obedience of the heart, which Pharaoh's terror was not; that Moses both knew this of himself and was told it by God, that the present submission was no real conversion; the warning in it, that God's ministers may discern, and must sometimes plainly tell, the hypocrisy of a confession that springs only from fear; that true fear of God is a thing of the heart, not extorted by terror).

themes: But as for thee and thy servants
I know that ye will not yet fear the LORD God
Moses seeing through Pharaoh's fair words to the unchanged heart
true fear of God is a thing of the heart, not extorted by terror

schools.summary: Gill: the flax and barley were smitten with the hail and destroyed, of which there were great quantities in Egypt, famous for its linen; the witnesses note the flax and barley being smitten, for the barley was in the ear and the flax was bolled — the particular note of the crops destroyed, marking the season and the loss (the Scripture recording just which harvests were ruined: 'the flax and the barley was smitten: for the barley was in the ear, and the flax was bolled'; the crops named, 'the flax and the barley,' important produce of Egypt, famous for its linen made from flax, beaten down and destroyed by the hail; the reason given, 'for the barley was in the ear, and the flax was bolled,' that these were then ripe or near ripe, the barley headed out, the flax in bud or boll, and so were full-grown and exposed, easily broken by the storm; that this exact note marks both the season of the year and the greatness of the loss to Egypt's economy; the truth and precision of the Scripture record, noting even which crops perished and why).

themes: And the flax and the barley was smitten
for the barley was in the ear, and the flax was bolled
the flax and barley smitten, full-grown and exposed to the storm
a particular note marking the season and the loss, the precision of the Scripture record

schools.summary: Gill: the wheat and rye were not smitten, the word rendered 'rye' being thought by some 'spelt' or 'rice'; they were not grown up; the witnesses note the wheat and rye being not smitten, because they were not yet grown up — the sparing of the later crops, leaving Egypt some provision (the hail sparing what was not yet exposed: 'the wheat and the rye were not smitten: for they were not grown up'; the crops spared, 'the wheat and the rye,' the latter perhaps spelt or rice, which were 'not grown up,' still low and tender, not yet headed out; the reason, 'for they were not grown up,' that, being still young and near the ground, they bent under the hail and were not broken as the tall, ripe flax and barley were; the mercy mingled even here, that not all Egypt's harvest was destroyed, the later crops being left, so that the land should not utterly perish of famine; the providence and measure in God's judgments, that he does not destroy utterly but leaves a remnant, tempering wrath with mercy; that God knows how to measure his strokes).

themes: But the wheat and the rye were not smitten
for they were not grown up
the wheat and rye spared, still low and tender, not broken as the ripe crops
the mercy mingled even here, God not destroying utterly but tempering wrath with mercy

schools.summary: Adam Clarke: Moses spread abroad his hands, probably with the rod of God in them, in the posture of earnest prayer; the witnesses note Moses going out of the city, spreading his hands to the LORD, and the thunders, hail, and rain ceasing — the prompt answer to prayer, the storm ending at God's word (the plague ceasing exactly as Moses had promised: 'Moses went out of the city... and spread abroad his hands unto the LORD: and the thunders and hail ceased, and the rain was not poured upon the earth'; the faithfulness of Moses, that he did as he had said, going out and praying; the posture, 'spread abroad his hands unto the LORD,' probably with the rod, the gesture of earnest intercession; the immediate answer, 'the thunders and hail ceased, and the rain was not poured upon the earth,' that at his prayer God at once stilled the whole storm, the heavens clearing as suddenly as they had broken; the proof in it, that the same God who sent the storm could end it at a word, hearing his servant's prayer; the power of the prayer of God's servant, and God's faithfulness to his promise).

themes: Moses went out of the city from Pharaoh, and spread abroad his hands unto the LORD
and the thunders and hail ceased, and the rain was not poured upon the earth
the storm ceasing at once at Moses' prayer, exactly as promised
the same God who sent the storm ending it at a word, hearing his servant's prayer

schools.summary: Gill: instead of being softened as he seemed under the plague, his heart became harder when delivered from it; the witnesses note Pharaoh, when the rain, hail, and thunder ceased, sinning yet more and hardening his heart, he and his servants — the relapse of the delivered, the mercy abused to fresh hardening (the removal of the plague hardening rather than humbling him: 'when Pharaoh saw that the rain and the hail and the thunders were ceased, he sinned yet more, and hardened his heart, he and his servants'; the cause of the relapse, 'when Pharaoh saw that the rain and the hail and the thunders were ceased,' that, the terror over and the danger past, his fear left him and his false repentance vanished; the increase of sin, 'he sinned yet more, and hardened his heart,' that, instead of being softened by the mercy of deliverance, he grew harder than before, adding the sin of broken promise to all the rest; that 'he and his servants' joined in the hardening, the whole court returning to their obstinacy; the dreadful truth the commentators mark, that mercies abused harden the heart, and that deliverance from judgment, if it does not humble, hardens; the difference between the godly, softened by mercy, and the wicked, hardened by it).

debates.question: What does Pharaoh's hardening when the storm ceased teach?

sides.position: That mercies abused harden the heart — deliverance from judgment, if it does not humble, hardens; with the terror past, Pharaoh's false repentance vanished and he sinned yet more, the difference between the godly softened by mercy and the wicked hardened by it

themes: when Pharaoh saw that the rain and the hail and the thunders were ceased
he sinned yet more, and hardened his heart, he and his servants
Pharaoh's false repentance vanishing with the terror, the mercy abused to fresh hardening
mercies abused harden the heart, deliverance hardening where it does not humble

schools.summary: Gill: his heart was hardened and he would not let Israel go, though he had so absolutely promised it, the word of the LORD by Moses being fulfilled; the witnesses note Pharaoh's heart being hardened, so that he would not let Israel go, as the LORD had spoken by Moses — the broken promise and the foretold hardening confirmed (Pharaoh, hardened, breaking his word and the prophecy standing: 'the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, neither would he let the children of Israel go; as the LORD had spoken by Moses'; the settled hardness, 'the heart of Pharaoh was hardened,' that his heart, far from being softened by the storm or the deliverance, was now fixed in its obstinacy; the broken promise, 'neither would he let the children of Israel go,' that, despite his absolute promise made under the storm, he would not perform it when the danger was past, false to his own word; the fulfilment, 'as the LORD had spoken by Moses,' that this very hardening and refusal had been foretold, so that even Pharaoh's faithless obstinacy confirmed the word of God; that God's purpose stood firm through all Pharaoh's broken promises and renewed hardness).

themes: And the heart of Pharaoh was hardened, neither would he let the children of Israel go
as the LORD had spoken by Moses
Pharaoh's heart fixed in hardness, breaking his absolute promise
his faithless obstinacy confirming the word of God, his purpose standing firm
